title: Controlled Vocabulary: Genre Depth Graph Node Roles
description: Canonical set of node roles used across all 27 genre depth graphs. Every node must be assigned exactly one role. The first five roles form the five-level spine defined in v0_plan.md §3.3. The additional roles handle structural concepts that appear across multiple levels.

node_roles.definition: The emotional and experiential contract with the audience — what the genre commits to delivering.
node_roles.structural_function: Root node. Every genre graph has exactly one. All other nodes trace back to this promise.
node_roles.examples_across_genres: Fear and dread (Horror), Solvable puzzle with fair resolution (Mystery), Wonder and mythic stakes (Fantasy)

node_roles.definition: A non-negotiable rule that must hold for the genre to function — what must be true in any story claiming this genre.
node_roles.structural_function: Direct children of Genre Promise. Define the minimum viable genre contract. Typically 3–6 per graph.
node_roles.examples_across_genres: Threat must be present and escalating (Horror), Fair clueing required (Mystery), Physical confrontation must resolve the central conflict (Action)

node_roles.definition: A recognized subtype with its own distinct constraint profile that inherits from but modifies the core constraints.
node_roles.structural_function: Branching layer. Each subgenre carries forward core constraints while adding or adjusting requirements. Typically 3–6 per graph.
node_roles.examples_across_genres: Cozy vs. Hardboiled vs. Police Procedural (Mystery), Space Opera vs. Cyberpunk vs. Hard SF (Science Fiction), Slasher vs. Supernatural vs. Cosmic (Horror)

node_roles.definition: A world-building or setting requirement that concretely expresses the genre — how the story's environment must behave.
node_roles.structural_function: Makes genre constraints tangible through setting mechanics. Specifies what must remain consistent in the story's world.
node_roles.examples_across_genres: Magic system must have costs and limits (Fantasy), Period-accurate social norms and technology (Historical), Isolation amplifies threat (Horror)

node_roles.definition: A specific, recurring scene type or beat that the audience expects and the genre contract demands.
node_roles.structural_function: Terminal or near-terminal nodes. The most concrete and testable layer — can be checked against a draft.
node_roles.examples_across_genres: First scare or anomaly (Horror), Investigation pivot scene (Mystery), Declaration or commitment scene (Romance)

node_roles.definition: A required atmospheric, emotional, or stylistic quality that the genre mandates — not a plot element but a register the story must sustain.
node_roles.structural_function: Can appear at Levels 2–4. Constrains how the story feels rather than what happens. Often connects to multiple scene obligations.
node_roles.examples_across_genres: Sustained dread atmosphere (Horror), Wit and verbal sparring (Romantic Comedy), Grandeur and awe (Fantasy)

node_roles.definition: An explicit prohibition — an approach, trope, or element that violates the genre contract if present.
node_roles.structural_function: Can appear at Levels 2–5. Defines the genre by exclusion. Useful for distinguishing similar genres and preventing common mistakes.
node_roles.examples_across_genres: Unsolvable puzzle or withheld clues (Mystery), Consequence-free violence (War), Unearned happy ending (Tragedy)
